items.caption:
Lies you absolutely cannot lie about on your resume otherwise you will lose your job offer. 

I talk about this and more in my latest YouTube video. Comment 'YouTube' for a direct link to the channel or check out my link in bio
items.transcript: I'm going to walk you through what you absolutely cannot lie about on your resume. And these lies will get your offer pulled the moment they're caught, and most of them get caught. Your education, your degree, whether you actually graduated. Legally claiming you have one when you don't counts as falsifying the application. The companies you worked at and the dates that you worked there. And if your dates don't line up to what your old employer reports, that's the end of the conversation. Whether you were fired or laid off. Getting fired isn't a deal-breaker. Lying about it is the deal-breaker. Skills and softwares you don't actually know how to use. Especially if you're at a senior level. Foreign language proficiency. There's a real chance an interviewer is going to start speaking it to you in your interview. So only list languages that you're actually fluent in. And finally, a fabricated job title or management responsibility. There's a difference between translating and inventing. Calling yourself CEO when you weren't is inventing. Those are the things you cannot lie about.

items.caption: Resumes are simple, they're a document that demonstrates your business value to a business. That's it!
items.transcript: What your resume is there to demonstrate is the business value of your labor. It's not a to-do list. Yeah, those are tasks, but what are the results of the things that you've done? Right now, I am hiring and I've been looking at a lot of resumes and a lot of it's just to-do lists. Also, the work that they've done, the most recent roles that they've done are not aligned to what I'm hiring for. And I'm being very clear in my job description exactly what it is that I'm hiring for. It demonstrates to the other party, can you communicate your labor value for this job to the hiring team?

items.caption: You're not a pick me, don't act like one! Its all about alignment
items.transcript: One thing that I say almost every single meeting that I have with a client is that you are not a pick-me. You are not Meredith Grey in Grey's Anatomy. There's no pick-me, choose-me, love-me. Let's just like take that off the table, okay? You are not there to be picked or to be chosen. Instead, if I can encapsulate job search and getting a promotion into one word, it's alignment. If you are not aligning your skills to what a job is looking for, for what a hiring manager is looking for, they're not going to hire you. Your mindset always needs to go back to alignment, alignment, alignment, alignment. Align your skills to what they need. Don't talk about things that are irrelevant. Talk about what they care about, not what you care to talk about. If you give them what they want, you get what you want. That's how it works.
